description: Dissented, argued that Article XI of the Texas Constitution provides an exemption for property "devoted exclusively to the use and benefit of the public," which the charter school's campus satisfies, regardless of fee ownership.

name: Chief Justice Hecht
description: Dissented with majority opinion.

name: Justice Huddle
description: Dissented with majority opinion.

name: Justice Busby
description: Concurred, argued that academic dismissal for poor performance does not carry sufficient stigma to implicate a protected liberty interest, and that the university provided adequate procedural due course of law.

name: Justice Massengale
description: Concurred with the judgment based on precedent, but questioned whether Texas courts should interpret the Constitution to protect a liberty interest in a student's reputation associated with the pursuit of graduate education.

name: Chief Justice Hecht
description: Concurred, argued that civil courts have jurisdiction over preemption claims even when local ordinances carry criminal penalties, and that TPGA has standing to bring its singular preemption claim.

name: Justice Guzman
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Justice Lehrmann
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Justice Devine
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Justice Busby
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Justice Bland
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Justice Huddle
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Justice Blacklock
description: Concurred in part and dissented in part, argued that while TPGA adequately pleaded injury for some regulations, it lacked standing for an omnibus challenge to all regulations without showing injury for each specific provision.

name: Justice Boyd
description: Concurred in part and dissented in part, agreeing with Justice Blacklock's opinion regarding TPGA's standing.

name: P.J. Keller
description: Concurred, argued that an appeal suspends the obligation to pay court costs, making the assessment of the time payment fee premature.

name: Hervey
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Richardson
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Newell
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Walker
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Yeary
description: Dissented, argued that the time payment fee is a legitimate court cost and previous rulings (*Peraza*, *Salinas*) on separation of powers were incorrect.

name: Keel
description: Dissented with majority opinion.

name: Slaughter
description: Dissented with majority opinion.

name: McClure
description: Dissented with majority opinion.

name: Jill J. Karofsky
description: Concurred, argued that the legislature's power to alter or suspend common law causes of action means that the right to a jury trial does not attach when such causes of action are abrogated, thus § 895.4801 does not violate the constitutional jury trial right.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Concurred, argued that Ramirez's speedy trial right was not violated because the lengthy delay was mainly due to neutral reasons, Ramirez asserted his right late, and he failed to show specific prejudice, also overruling the 'cavalier disregard' standard for delay attribution.

name: Ziegler
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Dallet
description: Concurred, but did not join specific paragraphs of the majority opinion due to their adversarial tone.

name: Hagedorn
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Karofsky
description: Concurred, emphasizing the importance of balancing victims' rights alongside those of the State and defendants in speedy trial cases.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Concurred, but argued the majority's analytical shortcut creates an overly exacting standard for defendants to prove speedy trial violations, giving too much weight to the defendant's delay in asserting their right and too little to the accumulation of neutral delays attributable to the State.

name: Janet C. Protasiewicz
description: Concurred with Chief Justice Ann Walsh Bradley's opinion.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Concurred, argued that the governor exceeded constitutional boundaries by partially vetoing a non-appropriation bill and that the legislature's appropriation of funds to JCF, not DPI, was proper.

name: Brian Hagedorn
description: Concurred, argued that the power to settle civil enforcement actions and cases brought at the request of executive-branch agencies falls within the core executive powers, and the legislative approval requirement unconstitutionally violates the separation of powers.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Concurred, arguing that the case did not belong before the court and the initial petition should not have been granted, but that the court's role is to decide important legal matters.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Rebecca Frank Dallet
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Janet C. Protasiewicz
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Dissented from the court's initial decision to grant the original action petition, arguing it was an improper exercise of political power and should not have been decided.

name: Brian Hagedorn
description: Dissented from the court's initial decision to grant the original action petition, arguing the court's actions were perplexing, outcome-oriented, and bypassed normal litigation.

name: Rebecca Frank Dallet
description: Concurred, delivering the majority opinion which held that comprehensive legislation enacted over 50 years impliedly repealed the 19th-century near-total abortion ban in Wisconsin.

name: Jill J. Karofsky
description: Concurred, emphasizing the severe and deadly consequences of abortion restrictions, advocating for the protection of women's health and well-being, and asserting the majority's decision shielded women from such consequences.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Janet Protasiewicz
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Dissented, arguing the majority's decision was an exercise of judicial will that usurped the legislature's role, applied a flawed interpretation of implied repeal, and allowed personal preferences to override legal analysis, thereby undermining the constitutional order.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Dissented, arguing the majority engaged in judicial activism by erasing a disliked law, distorting history, and undermining democratic principles, and also joined Justice Hagedorn's dissent.

name: Brian K. Hagedorn
description: Dissented, arguing the court unconstitutionally deleted a duly enacted law by misapplying the implied repeal doctrine, ignoring clear legislative intent to preserve the statute, and engaging in judicial policymaking.

name: Jill J. Karofsky
description: Concurred, argued that JCRAR's statutes violate bicameralism and presentment by altering legal rights and duties outside the legislative branch without proper lawmaking procedures.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Rebecca Frank Dallet
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Anthony O. Protasiewicz
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Brian K. Hagedorn
description: Concurred in part and dissented in part, argued that JCRAR's indefinite objection to the commercial building code rule was unconstitutional based on existing precedent, but dissented from the broad facial ruling as an overreach based on deficient arguments and creating more questions than answers.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Dissented, argued that the majority's decision unbalances state government, overrules decades of precedent without textual analysis of the Wisconsin Constitution, and inconsistently applies constitutional principles, empowering the executive branch at the expense of the legislative.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Dissented, argued the majority's decision transfers power from elected representatives to unelected bureaucrats, undermines the nondelegation principle, and ignores the Wisconsin Constitution's text, thereby diminishing the People's liberty and sovereignty.

name: Jill J. Karofsky
description: Concurred, arguing that the governor's partial vetoes were constitutional, satisfying all four deletion-veto principles, and that striking digits to create new numbers does not violate the constitution's prohibition against creating new words from letters.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Rebecca Frank Dallet
description: Concurred, arguing that the petitioners' substantive-part analysis, which claims the vetoed result must be "less than" the original, is incompatible with prior case law and should be rejected, thus upholding the vetoes as consistent with precedent.

name: Janet C. Protasiewicz
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Brian Hagedorn
description: Dissented, arguing that the governor's partial veto power was abused to unilaterally create new law, transforming a two-year tax increase into a 400-year one, which is repugnant to the constitutional structure and legislative lawmaking authority.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Dissented with majority opinion.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Dissented with majority opinion.

name: Jill J. Karofsky
description: Concurred, argued that Kenneth Brown lacked standing because he did not suffer a legally recognized personal injury from the WEC's decision, as required to be "aggrieved" under state law.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Concurred with the majority opinion.

name: Dallet
description: Concurred with the majority opinion.

name: Protasiewicz
description: Concurred with the majority opinion.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Dissented, argued that Brown had standing because he possesses a legal right to ensure local election officials comply with the law, and the alleged failure to conduct elections lawfully harmed that right.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Dissented, argued that the majority misinterprets the term "aggrieved" by not recognizing that a complainant who loses before WEC in an election matter has a statutory right to judicial review.

name: Brian Hagedorn
description: Dissented, argued that the legislature conferred a legal right upon voters to ensure local election officials comply with election laws, which should grant standing for judicial review.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Concurred, argued the prosecutor's statements describing the State's evidence as "uncontroverted" were not comments on the defendant's silence but rather directed the jury to consider only the evidence presented at trial, thus not violating Fifth Amendment rights.

name: Patience Drake Roggensack
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Concurred with majority opinion and with Justice Hagedorn's separate concurrence.

name: Brian Hagedorn
description: Concurred, argued the majority's faithful application of existing Supreme Court precedent and the Fifth Amendment's original meaning support the decision not to further extend the prohibition against prosecutor comments on a defendant's silence.

name: Karofsky
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Rebecca Frank Dallet
description: Dissented, argued the prosecutor's repeated characterization of evidence as "uncontroverted" where only the defendant could rebut it, constituted an indirect comment on the defendant's decision not to testify, violating Fifth Amendment rights.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Dissented with Justice Dallet's opinion.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Concurred, arguing that Wis. Stat. § 6.87(4)(b)1 allows the use of ballot drop boxes and that the previous Teigen ruling was unsound in principle and should be overruled.

name: Dallet
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Karofsky
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Protasiewicz
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Dissented, arguing that the majority abused the rule of law by overturning Teigen and replaced a reasonable interpretation of the law with a politically motivated decision.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Dissented with minority opinion.

name: Brian Hagedorn
description: Dissented with minority opinion.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Concurred, argued that both the motivations and activities of an organization must be examined to determine if it is operated primarily for religious purposes, and found that CCB's activities were primarily charitable and secular, not religious.

name: Dallet
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Karofsky
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Protasiewicz
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Dissented, argued that the majority misinterprets the statute by adding an "activities" requirement, resulting in unconstitutional religious discrimination, excessive government entanglement in religious affairs, and a violation of the Free Exercise Clause.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Dissented, argued that the majority misinterprets the statute, leading to unconstitutional religious discrimination and excessive government entanglement.

name: Brian Hagedorn
description: Dissented, agreed with the statutory construction in Justice Rebecca Grassl Bradley's dissent and the critique of the majority's reliance on the remedial statute canon, but did not reach constitutional questions.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Concurred with the majority opinion, affirming that the Transportation Utility Fee is a property tax subject to municipal levy limits, and further argued that the court should have also addressed the plaintiff's Uniformity Clause claim and that the fee violates the Uniformity Clause.

name: Patience Drake Roggensack
description: Concurred with the majority opinion and joined Judge Bradley's concurring opinion, agreeing that the court should have addressed the Uniformity Clause claim and that the fee violates it.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Concurred, arguing that WEC's memos authorizing ballot drop boxes and agent delivery of absentee ballots are unlawful because they conflict with Wisconsin statutes requiring personal delivery or mailing by the elector to the municipal clerk's office or a designated alternate site. She also argued that WEC's memos should have been formally promulgated as administrative rules.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Concurred with majority opinion and Justice Rebecca Grassl Bradley's concurring opinion.

name: Patience Drake Roggensack
description: Concurred, arguing that only the elector shall mail the absentee ballot to the municipal clerk, emphasizing the personal nature of ballot return.

name: Brian Hagedorn
description: Concurred, finding that WEC's memos are invalid because unstaffed drop boxes are not permitted and personal delivery requires a person-to-person exchange with the municipal clerk or an authorized representative at the clerk's office or designated alternate site. He disagreed with the majority's standing analysis, but found Teigen had standing based on Wis. Stat. § 5.06.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Dissented, arguing that the majority's decision creates an unnecessary barrier to voting, especially for rural and disabled voters, by prohibiting drop boxes and agent delivery, and that the majority's statutory interpretation is flawed and sows distrust in elections.

name: Rebecca Frank Dallet
description: Dissented with Justice Ann Walsh Bradley's dissenting opinion.

name: Jill J. Karofsky
description: Dissented with Justice Ann Walsh Bradley's dissenting opinion.

name: Jill J. Karofsky
description: Concurred and delivered the majority opinion, holding that legislative districts must be physically contiguous and that current maps violate this, while also stating the "least change" approach is unworkable and overruling previous applications of it.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Concurred with the majority opinion.

name: Dallet
description: Concurred with the majority opinion.

name: Protasiewicz
description: Concurred with the majority opinion.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Dissented, arguing that the majority's decision is judicial activism, improperly overrules recent precedent on redistricting, and misapplies legal doctrines like stare decisis, judicial estoppel, and laches due to political motivations.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Dissented, arguing that the majority's decision is a partisan power grab that disregards established legal precedent and procedures, and that the current maps were previously deemed contiguous by the court.

name: Brian Hagedorn
description: Dissented, arguing that the court's decision is politically motivated, improperly overturns a resolved issue, and abandons established legal principles like finality in litigation, standing, and stare decisis.

name: Janet C. Protasiewicz
description: Did not participate in the decision.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Concurred, argued that while the denial of the motion was correct, the court's new majority has abandoned precedent and uses its power for partisan agendas, harming the court's independence and integrity regarding redistricting cases.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Concurred, agreeing with Justice Bradley's opinion that while the denial of the motion was correct, the court's new majority has abandoned precedent and used its power for partisan agendas, harming the court's independence and integrity.

name: Rebecca Frank Dallet
description: Concurred, arguing that Shiffra was unsound in principle, unworkable in practice, and detrimental to the coherence of the law, warranting its overruling.

name: Janice S. Roggensack
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Brian Hagedorn
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Jill J. Karofsky
description: Concurred, arguing that Shiffra created an unworkable dilemma for victims by compromising their privacy and undermining the therapist-patient relationship.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Concurred, arguing that Shiffra objectively misinterpreted federal due process rights and usurped legislative power, criticizing the majority's use of public policy arguments as a basis for overturning precedent.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Dissented, arguing that Shiffra provided a workable and well-established procedure for balancing defendants' and victims' rights, and the majority wrongly disregarded stare decisis and misapplied its factors.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Dissented, arguing that Shiffra provided a workable and well-established procedure for balancing defendants' and victims' rights, and the majority wrongly disregarded stare decisis and misapplied its factors.

name: Brian Hagedorn
description: Concurred, argued that Marsy's Law was validly ratified as the ballot question was not fundamentally counterfactual and involved a single general purpose, and separately argued for re-embracing the distinction between binding precedent and non-binding dicta.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Concurred, argued that the "every essential" test is incompatible with the political question doctrine and that judicial power does not extend to all constitutional questions.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Concurred, joined Justice Rebecca Grassl Bradley's concurrence.

name: Patience Drake Roggensack
description: Concurred, joined Justice Rebecca Grassl Bradley's concurrence.

name: Rebecca Frank Dallet
description: Concurred, argued that Marsy's Law was validly adopted but criticized the majority's narrow interpretation of "submitted to the people" and its originalist methodology, advocating for a test requiring ballot questions to accurately summarize significant changes.

name: Jill J. Karofsky
description: Concurred, joined Justice Rebecca Frank Dallet's concurrence.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Dissented, argued the majority wrongly dismissed the established "every essential" test from Ekern and that the ballot question failed to inform voters about the diminution of criminal defendants' rights, thereby undermining democracy.

name: Brian Hagedorn
description: Concurred, argued that the motion for reconsideration did not meet the court's standards and defended his original 'text-and-history' approach to nondelegation as valid, noting petitioners failed to present an originalist case.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Dissented, argued that the majority's denial of reconsideration perpetuated errors in statutory interpretation, undermined the constitutional separation of powers, and allowed unelected bureaucrats to exercise legislative authority, thus eroding democratic norms.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Dissented, argued that the majority erred in its interpretation, joining Justice Bradley's dissent.

name: Patience Drake Roggensack
description: Dissented, argued that the majority erred in its interpretation, joining Justice Bradley's dissent.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Concurred, argued that the affidavit fulfilled the oath or affirmation requirement because the officer's actions and the document's language sufficiently demonstrated an obligation to tell the truth, prioritizing substance over form.

name: Patience Roggensack
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Brian Hagedorn
description: Concurred, argued that the Fourth Amendment requirement for an oath or affirmation was met as the affiant made a statement affirming truth under circumstances that impressed a solemn obligation, though it was a close call.

name: Jill J. Karofsky
description: Concurred, argued that the Fourth Amendment requirement for an oath or affirmation was met as the affiant made a statement affirming truth under circumstances that impressed a solemn obligation, though it was a close call.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Dissented, argued that the oath or affirmation requirement is a constitutional imperative, not merely a matter of good practice or subjective intent, and the affidavit's false assertion of being 'duly sworn' meant no actual oath was taken.

name: Rebecca Frank Dallet
description: Dissented, argued that the oath or affirmation requirement is a constitutional imperative, not merely a matter of good practice or subjective intent, and the affidavit's false assertion of being 'duly sworn' meant no actual oath was taken.

name: BRIAN HAGEDORN
description: Concurred, argued that the circuit court did not erroneously exercise its discretion by requiring parents to disclose their identities to opposing attorneys while allowing them to keep their names sealed from the public and the district, and declined to rule on the merits of the underlying policy or grant an injunction at this preliminary stage.

name: ANN WALSH BRADLEY
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: DALLET
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: KAROFSKY
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: PATIENCE DRAKE ROGGENSACK
description: Dissented, argued that the majority abdicated its responsibility by failing to address the fundamental constitutional right of parents to raise their children as they see fit and erred by not granting a temporary injunction against the school district's policy.

name: ANNETTE KINGSLAND ZIEGLER
description: Dissented with Justice Roggensack's opinion.

name: REBECCA GRASSL BRADLEY
description: Dissented with Justice Roggensack's opinion.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Concurred, argued that Prehn lawfully retains his position as a holdover on the DNR Board because his term's expiration did not create a vacancy under state statutes, and he is entitled to "for cause" protection.

name: Patience D. Roggensack
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Brian Hagedorn
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Rebecca Frank Dallet
description: Dissented, argued that Prehn's office became vacant upon the expiration of his term, and the majority's holding creates an absurd outcome that undermines the separation of powers and allows an unelected official to dictate their term.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Dissented with majority opinion.

name: Jill J. Karofsky
description: Dissented with majority opinion.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Concurred, argued that the local health officer lacked statutory authority to close schools and that the order violated the petitioners' fundamental right to free exercise of religion.

name: Ziegler
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Roggensack
description: Concurred with majority opinion.

name: Brian Hagedorn
description: Concurred, argued that the majority correctly interpreted the statutes and applied religious liberty protections, but expressed reservations about the majority's reasoning for addressing constitutional questions when statutory grounds were sufficient.

name: Rebecca Frank Dallet
description: Dissented, argued that the local health officer had statutory authority to close schools and that the majority's constitutional analysis was unnecessary and flawed.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Dissented with majority opinion.

name: Jill J. Karofsky
description: Dissented with majority opinion.

name: Patience Drake Roggensack
description: Concurred, argued that VanBeek was not seized when the officer initially took her license for a records check, but was unlawfully seized when the officer returned, withheld her license, and continued questioning to detain her until a K9 unit arrived, without reasonable suspicion.

name: Rebecca Frank Dallet
description: Concurred, argued that VanBeek was unlawfully seized both when the officer took her license to his squad car for a records check and when he returned to her vehicle, as a reasonable person would not feel free to leave in either scenario.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Concurred with the majority's decision that VanBeek was unlawfully seized and joined Justice Dallet's concurring opinion.

name: Jill J. Karofsky
description: Concurred with the majority's decision that VanBeek was unlawfully seized and joined Justice Dallet's concurring opinion.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Dissented, argued that VanBeek was not seized when the officer took her license to his squad car nor when he returned and continued questioning, as VanBeek remained free to ask for her license back and terminate the consensual encounter.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Dissented with majority opinion, joining Chief Justice Ziegler's dissenting opinion.

name: Brian K. Hagedorn
description: Dissented with majority opinion, joining Chief Justice Ziegler's dissenting opinion.

name: Brian Hagedorn
description: Concurred, delivering the majority opinion that rejected a per se rule for Miranda custody for incarcerated individuals and found Halverson not in custody under the totality of circumstances, thus denying the motion to suppress his statements.

name: Rebecca Grassl Bradley
description: Concurred, argued that the Knapp II decision, which expanded Wisconsin constitutional protections beyond federal ones, lacked textual or historical grounding and should be revisited.

name: Annette Kingsland Ziegler
description: Concurred, argued that the Knapp II decision, which expanded Wisconsin constitutional protections beyond federal ones, lacked textual or historical grounding and should be revisited, aligning with Justice Bradley.

name: Rebecca Frank Dallet
description: Concurred, argued that the Wisconsin Constitution provides greater protections than the federal constitution and criticized the majority for not upholding the state's tradition of independent interpretation, while agreeing with the outcome of this specific case.

name: Ann Walsh Bradley
description: Concurred, argued that the Wisconsin Constitution provides greater protections than the federal constitution and criticized the majority for not upholding the state's tradition of independent interpretation, aligning with Justice Dallet.

name: Jill J. Karofsky
description: Concurred, argued that the Wisconsin Constitution provides greater protections than the federal constitution and criticized the majority for not upholding the state's tradition of independent interpretation, aligning with Justice Dallet.
